blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلْمُرْسَلَـٰتِ عُرْفًا (١)
By the [angelic] emissaries sent successively, (1)
فَٱلْعَـٰصِفَـٰتِ عَصْفًا (٢)
by those who sweep along like gale, (2)
وَٱلنَّـٰشِرَٰتِ نَشْرًا (٣)
by those who publish [the Divine messages] far and wide, (3)
فَٱلْفَـٰرِقَـٰتِ فَرْقًا (٤)
by those who separate [the truth from falsehood] distinctly, (4)
فَٱلْمُلْقِيَـٰتِ ذِكْرًا (٥)
by those who inspire [God’s] remembrance, (5)
عُذْرًا أَوْ نُذْرًا (٦)
as exemption or warning: (6)
إِنَّمَا تُوعَدُونَ لَوَٰقِعٌ (٧)
what you are promised will surely befall. (7)
فَإِذَا ٱلنُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ (٨)
When the stars are blotted out (8)
وَإِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ فُرِجَتْ (٩)
and the sky is cleft, (9)
وَإِذَا ٱلْجِبَالُ نُسِفَتْ (١٠)
when the mountains are scattered [like dust] (10)
وَإِذَا ٱلرُّسُلُ أُقِّتَتْ (١١)
and time is set for the apostles [to bear witness] (11)
لِأَىِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ (١٢)
—for what day has [all] that been set [to occur]? (12)
لِيَوْمِ ٱلْفَصْلِ (١٣)
For the Day of Judgement! (13)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ (١٤)
And what will show you what is the Day of Judgement!? (14)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١٥)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (15)

(Commentary)

The Surah mentions the Day of Decision (يوم الفصل), which is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة), confirming the report of its occurrence and coupling it with a severe threat (الوعيد) for those who deny it, as well as a warning (الإنذار) and glad tidings (التبشير) for others. The aspect of the threat predominates in the Surah, as His saying: وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (Woe to the deniers on that day!) is repeated ten times.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمُرْسَلاتِ عُرْفاً By the [angels] sent forth successively (1) and the verses following it until the completion of six verses, are an oath (إقسام) from Him, the Exalted, by matters expressed as "those sent forth" (المرسلات), then "the tempestuous" (العاصفات), then "the spreaders" (الناشرات), then "the separators" (الفارقات), then "the deliverers" (الملقيات) of a reminder (ذكراً), as an excuse (عذراً) or a warning (نذراً). The first two—namely, "those sent forth successively" (المرسلات عرفاً) and "the tempestuous ones" (العاصفات عصفاً)—if left to themselves without considering the context, would appear to refer to successive winds blowing violently. However, the last one—namely, "the deliverers of a reminder, as an excuse or a warning"—is almost explicit in referring to the angels (الملائكة) descending upon the messengers (الرسل), carrying the revelation (الوحي) of the message and delivering it to them to complete the proof (إتمام الحجة) or as a warning. The remaining attributes do not resist being interpreted in a manner consistent with this meaning.

To interpret all five attributes as referring to winds—as is apparent in "those sent forth" and "the tempestuous"—requires great affectation (تكلف) in justifying the remaining three attributes, especially the final one.

Similarly, interpreting "those sent forth" and "the tempestuous" as winds while interpreting the remaining three, or the last two, or only the final one as the angels of revelation (ملائكة الوحي) is problematic, as there is no apparent affinity between winds and the angels of revelation such that they would be paired in the oaths and organized into a single sequence. The various justifications offered for this are far-fetched meanings that do not occur to the mind at the opening of the discourse without prior notification.

Therefore, the correct approach is to disregard these opinions—which are numerous and nearly impossible to categorize—and interpret the mentioned entities as the angels of revelation, similar to their counterparts at the beginning of Surah al-Saffat: وَالصَّافَّاتِ صَفًّا فَالزَّاجِراتِ زَجْراً فَالتَّالِياتِ ذِكْراً By those [angels] set in ranks, (1) by those who drive away [the clouds] (2) by those who recite the Reminder (3) [Qarai: 37:1-3]. In the same vein is His saying, the Exalted: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَداً لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسالاتِ رَبِّهِمْ Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, (26) except to an apostle He approves of. Then He dispatches a sentinel before him and behind him, (27) so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord... [Qarai: 72:26-28].

So His saying: وَالْمُرْسَلاتِ عُرْفاً is an oath by them. The word ‘urf (العرف) refers to the hair growing on the neck of a horse, and it is used as a metaphor for things when they follow one another; it is said: "They came like the mane of a horse" (كعرف الفرس). It is also used metaphorically to say: "The sandgrouse came ‘urfan" (عرفاً), meaning successively, and "They came to him as one ‘urf" (عرفاً واحداً), meaning in succession. Al-‘Urf (العرف) also means that which is "recognized" (المعروف) of commands and prohibitions. Thus, ‘urfan (عرفاً) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) according to the first meaning, and an object of purpose (مفعول له) according to the second. Sending (الإرسال) is the opposite of withholding (الإمساك). The feminine form of "those sent forth" (المرسلات) is used in consideration of the groups (الجماعات) or in consideration of the Spirit (الروح) with which the angels descend. He, the Exalted, said: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command onto whomever He wishes of His servants... [Qarai: 16:2], and He said: يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ ...He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants... [Qarai: 40:15].

The meaning is: I swear by the groups sent forth from the angels of revelation.

It has been said that "those sent forth successively" refers to the successive winds that are sent, but the weakness of this view has already been noted. Equally weak is the view that it refers to the Prophets (الأنبياء) عليه‌السلام, as it does not harmonize with what follows it.

His saying, the Exalted: فَالْعاصِفاتِ عَصْفاً by the tempestuous ones (2) is coordinated (عطف) with "those sent forth." The word ‘asf (العصف) refers to speed of travel, a metaphor derived from the "tempestuousness" (عصف) of winds—meaning the speed of their blowing—referring to the speed of their travel toward that for which they were sent. The meaning is: I swear by the angels who are sent successively and who hasten in their travel like tempestuous winds.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالنَّاشِراتِ نَشْراً by the spreaders (3) is another oath. To "spread" (نشر) a scroll, a book, a garment, or the like is to unfold it. What is meant by "spreading" here is the unfolding of the scrolls of revelation (صحف الوحي), as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا إِنَّها تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شاءَ ذَكَرَهُ فِي صُحُفٍ مُكَرَّمَةٍ مَرْفُوعَةٍ مُطَهَّرَةٍ بِأَيْدِي سَفَرَةٍ كِرامٍ بَرَرَةٍ No indeed! It is a reminder (11) —so let anyone who wishes remember it— (12) [recorded] in honored scrolls, (13) exalted and purified, (14) by the hands of envoys, (15) noble and pious. [Qarai: 80:11-16]. The meaning is: And I swear by the angels who spread the scrolls upon which the revelation is written for the Prophet to receive it.

It has been said that it refers to the winds that Allah, the Exalted, spreads before His mercy; or the winds that spread the clouds; or the angels who spread the scrolls of deeds (صحائف الأعمال); or the angels who spread their wings upon descending; and other things have been said as well.

His saying, the Exalted: فَالْفارِقاتِ فَرْقاً by the separators (4) refers to the separation (الفرق) between truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل), and between the lawful (الحلال) and the prohibited (الحرام). This "separation" is an attribute branching out from the aforementioned "spreading."

His saying, the Exalted: فَالْمُلْقِياتِ ذِكْراً عُذْراً أَوْ نُذْراً by the deliverers of the Reminder, (5) as an excuse or warning: (6) What is meant by the "Reminder" (الذكر) is the Quran which they recite to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, or revelation in general descending upon the Prophets to whom it is recited.

The three attributes—namely, spreading (النشر), separating (الفرق), and delivering the Reminder (إلقاء الذكر)—are sequential. The separation between truth and falsehood, and between the lawful and the prohibited, is realized through the spreading of the scrolls and the delivery of the Reminder. Through spreading, the separation begins to be realized, and through recitation, its realization is completed. Thus, spreading results in a degree of the existence of separation, and upon that follows the completion of its existence through delivery.

His saying: عُذْراً أَوْ نُذْراً (as an excuse or warning) are both objects of purpose (مفعول له), and "or" (أو) is for categorization (التنويع). It is said they are two verbal nouns (مصدران) meaning "excusing" (الإعذار) and "warning" (الإنذار). Excusing is to provide that by which one becomes excused (معذوراً). The meaning is that they deliver the Reminder so that it may be an excuse for His believing servants through the Reminder, and a frightening (تخويف) for others.

It has also been said: so that it may be an excuse by which Allah excuses Himself to His servants regarding punishment—that it was only done according to wisdom—which amounts to the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة). Thus, the sum of the meaning according to this is that they deliver the Reminder to be a completion of the proof against the deniers and a warning to others, which is a fine meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما تُوعَدُونَ لَواقِعٌ Indeed what you are promised will surely come to pass. (7) is the response to the oath (جواب القسم). The word "what" (ما) is a relative pronoun (موصولة), and the address is to all of humanity. What is meant by "what you are promised" is the Day of Resurrection with its punishment and reward. The word "occurring" (الواقع) is more emphatic than "being" (الكائن) because of the nuance of stability (الاستقرار) it carries. The meaning is that which Allah has promised you of resurrection, punishment, and reward will inevitably be realized.

( A Discourse on His Oaths in the Quran )

Among the subtle artistry of eloquence (صنعة البيان) in these six verses is that, while they contain oaths to emphasize the report in the response, they also contain the proof (الحجة) for the content of the response—namely, the occurrence of the promised recompense (الجزاء). For the Divine governance (التدبير الربوبي) to which the oath refers—namely, the sending of those sent forth tempestuously, their spreading of the scrolls, their separating, and their delivering the Reminder to the Prophet—is a governance that is not complete except with the existence of Divine obligation (التكليف الإلهي). And obligation is not complete except with the necessity of a day prepared for recompense, in which the disobedient and the obedient among the subjects of obligation are recompensed.

Thus, the very governance by which He, the Exalted, swore to emphasize the occurrence of the promised recompense is itself a proof of its occurrence. It is as if it were said: I swear by this proof that its implication is a reality.

If you contemplate the instances where the oath appears in His speech, the Exalted, and examine them deeply, you will find that the object of the oath (المقسم به) is a proof indicating the truth of the response (الجواب). For example, His saying regarding sustenance (الرزق): فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ By the Lord of the sky and the earth, it is indeed the truth... [Qarai: 51:23], for the Lordship (ربوبية) of the heaven and the earth is the principle (المبدأ) for the sustenance of those provided for. And His saying: لَعَمْرُكَ إِنَّهمْ لَفِي سَكْرَتِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ By your life, they were bewildered in their drunkenness. [Qarai: 15:72], for the pure life of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, protected by the infallibility (عصمة) of Allah, is indicative of their drunkenness and bewilderment. And His saying: وَالشَّمْسِ وَضُحاها By the sun and its morning brightness, (1) —until He said— وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها وَقَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها and by the soul and Him who fashioned it, (7) and inspired it with [discernment between] its virtues and vices: (8) one who purifies it is certainly felicitous, (9) and one who betrays it is certainly a failure. [Qarai: 91:1, 7-10]. For this precise system (النظام المتقن) that culminates in the inspired soul which distinguishes its vices and virtues is the evidence for the success of the one who purifies it and the failure of the one who corrupts it.

Following this pattern are the rest of the oaths mentioned in His speech, the Exalted, even if some of them are not without a degree of obscurity that requires deep reflection, such as His saying: وَالتِّينِ وَالزَّيْتُونِ وَطُورِ سِينِينَ By the Fig and the Olive, (1) by Mount Sinai, (2) [Qarai: 95:1-2]. It is upon you to contemplate them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذَا النُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ So when the stars are put out, (8) —until His saying— أُقِّتَتْ [when] the apostles are given their time, (11) is an exposition of the promised Day whose occurrence was reported in His saying: إِنَّما تُوعَدُونَ لَواقِعٌ (Indeed what you are promised will surely come to pass). The response to "when" (إذا) is omitted, indicated by His saying: لِأَيِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ For what day was [all this] deferred? (12) —until His saying— لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ Woe to the deniers on that day! (15).

He, the Glorified, has defined the promised Day by mentioning occurring events that are inseparable from the extinction of the human world and the cessation of the worldly system (النظام الدنيوي), such as the extinguishing of the stars, the splitting of the earth, the crumbling of the mountains, and the transformation of the system into another system that differs from it. This has been repeated in many Quranic Surahs, especially the short ones like Surah al-Naba, al-Nazi‘at, al-Takwir, al-Infitar, al-Inshiqaq, al-Fajr, al-Zilzal, al-Qari‘ah, and others. These mentioned matters have been counted in the narrations (الأخبار) as among the signs of the Hour (أشراط الساعة).

It is known by necessity (بالضرورة) from the expositions of the Book and the Sunnah that the system of life in all its affairs in the Hereafter (الآخرة) is different from its system in this world (الدنيا). The Abode of the Hereafter is an eternal abode (دار أبدية) containing pure felicity (السعادة) for its inhabitants—they have therein whatever they wish—or pure wretchedness (الشقاء), and they have therein only what they loathe. The worldly abode is an abode of transience and cessation (دار فناء وزوال) where only external, apparent causes and factors govern, where death is mixed with life, loss with finding, wretchedness with felicity, toil with rest, and sorrow with joy. The Hereafter is an abode of recompense (دار جزاء) and no action (عمل), while the world is an abode of action and no recompense. In short, the realm (النشأة) is different from the realm.

Thus, His defining the realm of resurrection and recompense by its signs—which involve the folding of the carpet of the world through the destruction of the structure of its earth, the uprooting of its mountains, the splitting of its sky, and the extinguishing of its stars, among other things—is like defining one realm by the collapse of the system governing another realm. He, the Exalted, said: وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُولى فَلَوْ لا تَذَكَّرُونَ Certainly you have known the first genesis, then why do you not take admonition? [Qarai: 56:62].

So His saying: فَإِذَا النُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ means their trace of light and other things is erased. Al-Tams (الطمس) is the removal of a trace by erasing. He, the Exalted, said: وَإِذَا النُّجُومُ انْكَدَرَتْ when the stars are dimmed, [Qarai: 81:2].

And His saying: وَإِذَا السَّماءُ فُرِجَتْ when the sky is split open, (9) means it is rent asunder. Al-Farj (الفرج) and al-Furjah (الفرجة) is the gap between two things. He, the Exalted, said: إِذَا السَّماءُ انْشَقَّتْ When the sky is split open, [Qarai: 84:1].

And His saying: وَإِذَا الْجِبالُ نُسِفَتْ when the mountains are scattered [as dust], (10) means they are uprooted and removed, from the saying: "The wind nasafat (نسفت) the thing," meaning it uprooted and removed it. He, the Exalted, said: وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْجِبالِ فَقُلْ يَنْسِفُها رَبِّي نَسْفاً They ask you concerning the mountains. Say, ‘My Lord will hollow them out [into dust]. [Qarai: 20:105].

And His saying: وَإِذَا الرُّسُلُ أُقِّتَتْ [when] the apostles are given their time, (11) means the time is appointed for them to be present to bear witness over the nations, or they have reached the time they were waiting for to deliver their testimony over the nations, from ta’qit (التأقيت) meaning tawqit (التوقيت) — the appointment of a specific time. He, the Exalted, said: فَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْئَلَنَّ الْمُرْسَلِينَ We will surely question those to whom [the apostles] were sent, and We will surely question the apostles. [Qarai: 7:6], and He said: يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ اللهُ الرُّسُلَ فَيَقُولُ ما ذا أُجِبْتُمْ The day Allah will gather the apostles and say, ‘What was the response you received?’... [Qarai: 5:109].

His saying, the Exalted: لِأَيِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ لِيَوْمِ الْفَصْلِ وَما أَدْراكَ ما يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ For what day was [all this] deferred? (12) For the Day of Decision. (13) And what will show you what the Day of Decision is? (14) Woe to the deniers on that day! (15) Al-Ajal (الأجل) is the period fixed for a thing, and al-ta’jil (التأجيل) is the setting of a term for a thing. It is used for its necessary consequence, which is "deferment" (التأخير), as in the saying: "a deferred debt" (دين مؤجل), meaning it has a term, as opposed to "immediate" (الحال). This meaning is most suitable for the verse. The pronoun in "deferred" (أُجِّلَتْ) refers to the matters mentioned previously: the extinguishing of the stars, the splitting of the sky, the uprooting of the mountains, and the timing of the messengers. The meaning is: For what day were these matters deferred?

It is possible that "deferred" (أُجِّلَتْ) means the fixing of a term for a thing, and that the implied pronoun in it refers to the messengers, or to what the speech suggests of matters related to the messengers—such as what they reported of the states of the Hereafter, its terrors, the punishment of the disbelievers, and the blessing of the believers therein. None of this is without some obscurity.

The verse and the one following it—namely, His saying: لِأَيِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ لِيَوْمِ الْفَصْلِ—are cast in the form of a question and its answer for the purpose of glorification (التعظيم), making it formidable (التهويل), and expressing wonder (التعجيب). The original meaning is: These matters were deferred for the Day of Decision.

This type of interrogative sentence is in the sense of an implied statement (تقدير القول). The meaning is that due to the greatness of this day, its terror, and its being a wonder, it is asked: For what day were these great, terrifying, and wondrous matters deferred? Then it is answered: For the Day of Decision.

His saying: لِيَوْمِ الْفَصْلِ is the Day of Recompense in which the judgment is decided (فصل القضاء). He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ اللهَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ ...Indeed Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection... [Qarai: 22:17].

His saying: وَما أَدْراكَ ما يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ is a glorification of the Day and an aggrandizement of its affair.

His saying: وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ — Al-Wayl (الويل) is destruction (الهلاك). What is meant by "the deniers" (المكذبين) are those who deny the Day of Decision in which there is what they are promised, for the verses are directed toward explaining its occurrence, and He has sworn that it is a reality.

In the verse is an invocation against the deniers, and it has made the mention of the response to "when" (إذا) in His saying: فَإِذَا النُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ etc., unnecessary. The estimation (التقدير) is: "When such and such happens, the punishment you were promised for denial will occur," or the estimation is: "When such and such happens, it will be the Day of Decision and the deniers of it will perish."

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Khisal, from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: Abu Bakr said: "Grayness has hastened to you, O Messenger of Allah." He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: شيبتني هود والواقعة والمرسلات وعم يتساءلون — "Surahs Hud, al-Waqi‘ah, al-Mursalat, and ‘Amma Yatasa’alun have made me gray."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Bukhari, Muslim, al-Nasa’i, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: "While we were with the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in a cave at Mina—when Surah wa al-Mursalat ‘Urfa was revealed to him—he was reciting it and I was receiving it from his mouth—and his mouth was still moist with it—when a snake leaped at him. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: اقتلوها — 'Kill it.' So we rushed toward it, but it escaped. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: وقيت شركم كما وقيتم شرها — 'It has been protected from your evil just as you have been protected from its evil.'"

I say: It has also been narrated through two other chains.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمُرْسَلاتِ عُرْفاً, he said: "Verses that follow one another."

In al-Majma‘: Regarding the verse, it was said: "They are the angels sent with the 'recognized' (المعروف)—of the command of Allah and His prohibition." This is in the narration of al-Harawi from Ibn Mas‘ud, and from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali from the companions of ‘Ali, from him عليه‌السلام.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذَا النُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ, he said: "Their light goes away and they fall."

In the same source, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: فَإِذَا النُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ: "Their extinguishing is the departure of their light." وَإِذَا السَّماءُ فُرِجَتْ: He said: "It opens and splits." وَإِذَا الرُّسُلُ أُقِّتَتْ: He said: "They were sent at different times."

In al-Majma‘, Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام said: "أُقِّتَتْ means they were sent at different times."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لِأَيِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ, he said: "Deferred."

Ayahs

أَلَمْ نُهْلِكِ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٦)
Did We not destroy the former generations, (16)
ثُمَّ نُتْبِعُهُمُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١٧)
[and] then made the latter ones follow them? (17)
كَذَٰلِكَ نَفْعَلُ بِٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (١٨)
That is how We deal with the guilty. (18)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١٩)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (19)
أَلَمْ نَخْلُقكُّم مِّن مَّآءٍ مَّهِينٍ (٢٠)
Have We not created you from a base fluid, (20)
فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ فِى قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ (٢١)
[and] then lodged it in a secure abode (21)
إِلَىٰ قَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (٢٢)
until a known span [of time]? (22)
فَقَدَرْنَا فَنِعْمَ ٱلْقَـٰدِرُونَ (٢٣)
Then We determined; and how excellent determiners We are! (23)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٢٤)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (24)
أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ ٱلْأَرْضَ كِفَاتًا (٢٥)
Have We not made the earth a receptacle (25)
أَحْيَآءً وَأَمْوَٰتًا (٢٦)
for the living and the dead, (26)
وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ شَـٰمِخَـٰتٍ وَأَسْقَيْنَـٰكُم مَّآءً فُرَاتًا (٢٧)
and set in it lofty [and] firm mountains, and given you agreeable water to drink? (27)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٢٨)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (28)
ٱنطَلِقُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَا كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ (٢٩)
[The faithless will be told:] ‘Proceed toward what you used to deny! (29)
ٱنطَلِقُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ ظِلٍّ ذِى ثَلَـٰثِ شُعَبٍ (٣٠)
Proceed toward the triple-forked shadow, (30)
لَّا ظَلِيلٍ وَلَا يُغْنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهَبِ (٣١)
neither shady nor of any avail against the flames. (31)
إِنَّهَا تَرْمِى بِشَرَرٍ كَٱلْقَصْرِ (٣٢)
Indeed it throws up [giant] sparks like castles, (32)
كَأَنَّهُۥ جِمَـٰلَتٌ صُفْرٌ (٣٣)
[bright] like yellow camels. (33)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٣٤)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (34)
هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ لَا يَنطِقُونَ (٣٥)
This is a day wherein they will not speak, (35)
وَلَا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ (٣٦)
nor will they be permitted to offer excuses. (36)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٣٧)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (37)
هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ ۖ جَمَعْنَـٰكُمْ وَٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٣٨)
‘This is the Day of Judgement. We have brought you together with the former generations. (38)
فَإِن كَانَ لَكُمْ كَيْدٌ فَكِيدُونِ (٣٩)
If you have any stratagems [left], try them out against Me!’ (39)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٤٠)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (40)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى ظِلَـٰلٍ وَعُيُونٍ (٤١)
Indeed the Godwary will be amid shades and springs (41)
وَفَوَٰكِهَ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ (٤٢)
and [enjoying] such fruits as they desire. (42)
كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ هَنِيٓـًٔۢا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٤٣)
[They will be told:] ‘Enjoy your food and drink, [a reward] for what you used to do. (43)
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٤٤)
Thus do We reward the virtuous.’ (44)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٤٥)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (45)
كُلُوا۟ وَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ قَلِيلًا إِنَّكُم مُّجْرِمُونَ (٤٦)
‘Eat and enjoy a little! You are indeed guilty.’ (46)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٤٧)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (47)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱرْكَعُوا۟ لَا يَرْكَعُونَ (٤٨)
When they are told, ‘Bow down [in prayer],’ they do not bow down! (48)
وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٤٩)
Woe to the deniers on that day! (49)
فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ بَعْدَهُۥ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٠)
So what discourse will they believe after this? (50)

thoughtful Standard mine technical terms like "God-conscious" and "Unseen" as per instructions.

(Commentary)

These are arguments## (Commentary)

These are arguments indicating the oneness of Lordship (توحد الربوبية) — the absolute uniqueness of Allah in His role as the Sustainer, Governor, and Regulator of all creation — which necessitate the existence of the Day of Sorting (يوم الفصل) — the Day of Judgment when truth is distinguished from falsehood — wherein lies the recompense for those who deny it. It also points to the prepared punishment for them, which they used to deny, and to the bliss and honor for the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness (تقوى) and remain ever-mindful of Allah’s boundaries. The section concludes by rebuking and censuring them for their arrogance in refusing to worship the Exalted and believe in His speech.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ نُهْلِكِ الْأَوَّلِينَ ثُمَّ نُتْبِعُهُمُ الْآخِرِينَ كَذلِكَ نَفْعَلُ بِالْمُجْرِمِينَ Did We not destroy the former generations? Then We will make the latter ones follow them. Thus do We deal with the guilty. (77:16-18). The interrogation is for the purpose of negation (الإنكار). What is meant by "the former generations" (الأولين) are those like the people of Noah, ʿAd, and Thamud among the nations of ancient times. "The latter ones" (الآخرين) refers to those who followed them from the nations of the past. "Following" (الإتباع) means making one thing come after another.

His saying: ثُمَّ نُتْبِعُهُمُ (Then We will make them follow) is with the nominative case (الرفع) on the verb nutbiʿuhum as a new sentence (الاستيناف), and it is not coordinated (معطوف) with nuhlik (destroy); otherwise, it would have been in the jussive case (الجزم).

The meaning is: We have already destroyed the deniers among the former nations, and then We shall destroy the latter nations in their wake.

His saying: كَذلِكَ نَفْعَلُ بِالْمُجْرِمِينَ (Thus do We deal with the guilty) is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for what preceded it. For this reason, it was mentioned as a separate sentence (الفصل) without a conjunction (عطف), as if a questioner asked: "Why were they destroyed?" and it was replied: "Thus do We deal with the guilty (المجرمين)." These verses—as you see—are a warning and a return of the explanation to the fundamental principle established in the Surah, namely His saying: وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (Woe to the deniers on that day!). This is itself an argument for the oneness of Lordship (توحد الربوبية); for the destruction of the guilty among mankind is an act of disposal (تصرف) and governance (تدبير) in the human world. Since none destroys except Allah—as the polytheists themselves admitted—He is the Lord (الرب), there is no lord besides Him and no god other than Him.

Furthermore, it indicates the existence of the Day of Sorting (يوم الفصل) because the destruction of a people for their guilt cannot occur except after a command (تكليف) has been directed toward them which they then disobey. There is no meaning to a command (التكليف) except with the rewarding of the obedient and the punishing of the disobedient. Thus, there must be a day in which the judgment is sorted, where the obedient are rewarded and the disobedient are punished. This is not the worldly reward and punishment, for they do not encompass everyone in this abode. Therefore, there must be a day in which everyone is recompensed for what they did, and that is the Day of Sorting (يوم الفصل); that is a day for which mankind will be gathered.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ نَخْلُقْكُمْ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ — to His saying — فَنِعْمَ الْقادِرُونَ Did We not create you from a mean water, then We lodged it in a secure abode, until a known term? So We determined; and how excellent Determinants are We! (77:20-23). The interrogation is for negation (الإنكار). The "mean water" (ماء مهين) is that which is lowly and of little utility, referring to the sperm-drop (النطفة). The "secure abode" (القرار المكين) refers to the womb (الرحم), and His saying "a known term" (قدر معلوم) refers to the period of pregnancy.

His saying: فَقَدَرْنا (So We determined) is from al-qadr, meaning ordainment or determination (التقدير). The letter fa (ف) indicates that the determination is a consequence of the creation; meaning: We created you and then We determined what would happen to you of events and what would face you of attributes and states, such as length or shortness of life, form, beauty, health, sickness, sustenance, and so on.

It has also been suggested that فَقَدَرْنا is from al-qudrah (power), as the opposite of incapacity (العجز), meaning: We had power over all of that. However, the previous explanation is more appropriate.

The meaning is: We created you from a lowly water, which is the sperm-drop, then We placed that water in a secure abode, which is the womb, for a known duration, which is the period of pregnancy. Then We determined everything related to your existence of events, attributes, and states; so how excellent are We as Determinants (المقدرون).

The content of these verses serves as an argument for the oneness of Lordship (توحد الربوبية) in a manner similar to the previous explanation. Likewise, it serves as an argument for the reality of the Day of Sorting (يوم الفصل), for Lordship necessitates the submission of the subjects to its presence, which is the religion (الدين) that encompasses the divine command (التكليف). The command is not complete except by establishing a recompense for obedience and disobedience, and the day in which deeds are recompensed is the Day of Sorting.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ الْأَرْضَ كِفاتاً أَحْياءً وَأَمْواتاً — to His saying — فُراتاً Did We not make the earth a receptacle for the living and the dead, and set in it high, firm mountains, and give you fresh water to drink? (77:25-27). The words al-kaft and al-kifat mean to draw together and gather (الضم والجمع); meaning: Did We not make the earth a gathering place (كفاتا) that joins the servants together, both the living and the dead? It is also said that al-kifat is the plural of kaft, meaning a vessel (الوعاء); thus the meaning would be: Did We not make the earth vessels that gather the living and the dead?

His saying: وَجَعَلْنا فِيها رَواسِيَ شامِخاتٍ (and set in it high, firm mountains). The rawasi are the firm mountains, and the shamikhat are the lofty ones. The mention of the firm mountains was a preamble to His saying: وَأَسْقَيْناكُمْ ماءً فُراتاً (and give you fresh water to drink), because rivers and natural springs gush forth from the mountains and flow onto the plains. Al-furat is sweet, fresh water.

The argumentation of these verses follows the same course as the previous explanation.

His saying, the Exalted: انْطَلِقُوا إِلى ما كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تُكَذِّبُونَ [It will be said,] ‘Proceed toward what you used to deny! (77:29). This is a narration of what will be said to them on the Day of Sorting. The speaker is Allah, the Glorified, as evidenced by His saying at the end of the verses: فَإِنْ كانَ لَكُمْ كَيْدٌ فَكِيدُونِ (so if you have any stratagem, use it against Me). What they used to deny is Hell (جهنم). "Proceeding" (الانطلاق) is moving from one place to another without lingering. The meaning is that it will be said to them: Move from the place of gathering (المحشر) without delay toward the Fire which you used to deny.

His saying, the Exalted: انْطَلِقُوا إِلى ظِلٍّ ذِي ثَلاثِ شُعَبٍ Proceed toward a triple-forked shadow (77:30). It has been mentioned that this shadow refers to the smoke of the fire of Hell. The Exalted said: وَظِلٍّ مِنْ يَحْمُومٍ (and the shadow of a black smoke) [Al-Waqiʿah: 43].

It has also been mentioned that the description of it branching into three forks (ثلاث شعب) indicates the enormity of the smoke, for massive smoke disperses like flowing locks of hair.

His saying, the Exalted: لا ظَلِيلٍ وَلا يُغْنِي مِنَ اللهَبِ neither shading, nor availing against the flames. (77:31). A "shading shadow" (الظل الظليل) is that which prevents heat and harm by covering the one seeking shade. Thus, the shadow being "not shading" means it does not prevent such harm. The "flame" (اللهب) is what rises above the fire in red, yellow, and green hues.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّها تَرْمِي بِشَرَرٍ كَالْقَصْرِ كَأَنَّهُ جِمالَتٌ صُفْرٌ Indeed it throws sparks like [huge] castles, as if they were yellow camels. (77:32-33). The pronoun "it" (إنها) refers to the Fire, known from the context. "Sparks" (الشرر) are what fly off from the fire. The "castle" (القصر) is well-known. Al-jimalah (جمالة) is the plural of jamal, which is a camel. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ وَلا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ This is a day they will not speak, nor will they be permitted to offer excuses. (77:35-36). The reference is to the Day of Sorting. The "permission" (الإذن) refers to permission to speak or to offer excuses.

His saying: فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ (to offer excuses) is coordinated (معطوف) with "permitted" (يؤذن) and is included within the scope of the negation. The meaning is: This day is a day in which they—the people gathered from mankind—shall not speak, nor will they be permitted to speak or offer excuses, so they will not offer excuses. The negation of speech here does not contradict its affirmation in other verses, because that Day has many different stations (مواقف); they will be questioned in some and thus speak, while their mouths will be sealed in others and they will not speak.

This was previously discussed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَأْتِ لا تَكَلَّمُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ (The day it comes, no soul shall speak except by His leave) [Hud: 105]; so let the reader refer back to it.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ جَمَعْناكُمْ وَالْأَوَّلِينَ فَإِنْ كانَ لَكُمْ كَيْدٌ فَكِيدُونِ ‘This is the Day of Sorting: We have gathered you and the former generations. So if you have any stratagem, use it against Me!’ (77:38-39). It is called the Day of Sorting (يوم الفصل) because Allah, the Exalted, sorts and distinguishes therein between the people of truth and the people of falsehood by judging between them. The Exalted said: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ) [As-Sajdah: 25], and He said: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ) [Yunus: 93].

The address in His saying: جَمَعْناكُمْ وَالْأَوَّلِينَ (We have gathered you and the former generations) is to the deniers of this nation, insofar as they are among the "latter ones"; hence they are contrasted with the "former ones." The Exalted said: ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ (That is a day for which mankind will be gathered) [Hud: 103], and He said: وَحَشَرْناهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَداً (and We shall muster them, and We will not leave out any one of them) [Al-Kahf: 47].

His saying: فَإِنْ كانَ لَكُمْ كَيْدٌ فَكِيدُونِ (So if you have any stratagem, use it against Me!) means: If you have any trick by which you can outmaneuver Me to ward off My punishment from yourselves, then use that trick. This is an address of incapacitation (تعجيزي), announcing the total stripping of power and ability from them on that day, through the manifestation that there is no power except with Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه). The Exalted said: وَلَوْ يَرَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِذْ يَرَوْنَ الْعَذابَ أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً وَأَنَّ اللهَ شَدِيدُ الْعَذابِ إِذْ تَبَرَّأَ الَّذِينَ اتُّبِعُوا مِنَ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوا وَرَأَوُا الْعَذابَ وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبابُ (That when the wrongdoers behold the punishment, [they will realize] that power altogether belongs to Allah, and that Allah is severe in punishment; when those who were followed will disown those who followed [them], and they sight the punishment and their means are cut off) [Al-Baqarah: 165-166].

The verse, namely His saying: فَإِنْ كانَ لَكُمْ كَيْدٌ فَكِيدُونِ, is broader in its implication than His saying: يا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَنْ تَنْفُذُوا مِنْ أَقْطارِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ فَانْفُذُوا لا تَنْفُذُونَ إِلَّا بِسُلْطانٍ (O company of jinn and humans! If you can pass through the confines of the heavens and the earth, then do pass! But you will not pass except by an authority [from Allah]) [Ar-Rahman: 33], because the latter is specific to the negation of the ability to flee. This is in contrast to the verse we are currently discussing. In His saying: فَكِيدُونِ (use it against Me), there is a shift (التفات) from the first-person plural (speaking with others) to the first-person singular (speaking alone). The subtle point (النكتة) in this is that the object of this command of incapacitation is the stratagem against the One who alone possesses power and ability, and that is Allah alone. Had it been said: "use it against Us" (فكيدونا), it would have missed this indication of absolute oneness (التوحد).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي ظِلالٍ وَعُيُونٍ وَفَواكِهَ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ — to His saying — الْمُحْسِنِينَ Indeed the God-conscious will be amid shades and springs, and fruits such as they desire. ‘Eat and drink agreeably for what you used to do.’ Thus do We reward the virtuous. (77:41-44). The "shades" (الظلال) and "springs" (العيون) are the shades and springs of Paradise, which they enjoy by seeking shade and drinking. "Fruits" (الفواكه) is the plural of fakiha, which is a fruit.

His saying: كُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا هَنِيئاً بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (Eat and drink agreeably for what you used to do) signifies permission and lawfulness (الإباحة). Eating and drinking here serve as a metaphor for all forms of enjoying the blessings of Paradise and utilizing them, even if it is not through literal eating and drinking. This is common usage, just as "consuming wealth" (أكل المال) is used for any form of utilizing it.

His saying: إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ (Thus do We reward the virtuous) is a confirmation of their felicity.

His saying, the Exalted: كُلُوا وَتَمَتَّعُوا قَلِيلاً إِنَّكُمْ مُجْرِمُونَ ‘Eat and enjoy yourselves for a little while; you are indeed guilty!’ (77:46). This address is like the saying: "Do what you wish, for it will not benefit you." This type of command is intended to make the addressee despair (إياس) of benefiting from the action they perform to achieve what they desire. Of this type is His saying: فَاقْضِ ما أَنْتَ قاضٍ إِنَّما تَقْضِي هذِهِ الْحياةَ الدُّنْيا (So decide what you will decide. You can only decide regarding the life of this world) [Taha: 72], and His saying: اعْمَلُوا ما شِئْتُمْ إِنَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (Do what you wish; indeed He watches what you do) [Fussilat: 40].

Thus, His saying: كُلُوا وَتَمَتَّعُوا قَلِيلاً (Eat and enjoy yourselves for a little while) means: a brief enjoyment or for a short time. It is to make them despair of benefiting from such eating and enjoyment in warding off the punishment from themselves; so let them eat and enjoy for a little while, for it will not ward off anything from them.

Eating and enjoyment were specifically mentioned because those who deny the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and the life hereafter — see no felicity except the felicity of the worldly life, and they see no part of that felicity except the success of eating and enjoyment, like brute animals. The Exalted said: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يَتَمَتَّعُونَ وَيَأْكُلُونَ كَما تَأْكُلُ الْأَنْعامُ وَالنَّارُ مَثْوىً لَهُمْ (As for the faithless, they enjoy themselves and eat like the cattle eat, and the Fire will be their abode) [Muhammad: 12].

His saying: إِنَّكُمْ مُجْرِمُونَ (you are indeed guilty) provides the reason (تعليل) for what is understood from the preceding sentence containing the command; meaning: eating and enjoying for a little while will not benefit you because you are guilty (مجرمون) due to your denial of the Day of Sorting, and the recompense for the deniers is inevitably the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ارْكَعُوا لا يَرْكَعُونَ When they are told, ‘Bow down,’ they do not bow down. (77:48). What is meant by "bowing" (الركوع) is prayer (الصلاة), as has been said, perhaps because prayer includes the act of bowing.

It has also been said that the "bowing" they are commanded to do is humility (الخشوع), submission (الخضوع), and modesty (التواضع) before the Exalted by responding to His call, accepting His speech, following His religion, and worshipping Him.

It has also been said that "bowing" refers to when they will be commanded to prostrate on the Day of Resurrection, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ (and they will be summoned to prostrate, but they will not be able to) [Al-Qalam: 42]. Both of these views are not without some far-fetchedness.

The connection of this verse to what preceded it is that the discourse was directed toward threatening the deniers of the Day of Sorting and explaining the consequence of their denial. This was completed in this verse by stating that they do not worship Allah when called to His worship, just as they deny that Day; for there is no meaning to worship while denying the recompense. This also serves as a preamble to His upcoming saying: فَبِأَيِّ حَدِيثٍ بَعْدَهُ يُؤْمِنُونَ (So in what discourse after this will they believe?).

It is attributed to al-Zamakhshari that the verse is connected to His saying in the previous verse: لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (to the deniers), as if it were said: Woe on that day to those who denied and those who, when told to bow down, do not bow down.

In the verse, there is a shift (التفات) from direct address to the third person (الغيبة) in His saying: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ (When they are told...). The reason for this is the turning away from addressing them after they were left to themselves to do as they please in His saying: كُلُوا وَتَمَتَّعُوا (Eat and enjoy yourselves).

His saying, the Exalted: فَبِأَيِّ حَدِيثٍ بَعْدَهُ يُؤْمِنُونَ So in what discourse after this will they believe? (77:50). Meaning: if they do not believe in the Quran, which is a miraculous divine sign (آية معجزة), and it has explained to them that Allah is the only God, alone without partner, and that before them is the Day of Sorting, with the clearest explanation and most radiant proof—then in what speech after the Quran will they believe?

This is to induce despair regarding their belief in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day. It is like a notification that ceasing the call to faith by addressing them with the words "Eat and enjoy yourselves" is appropriate, for they are not believers, and there is no benefit in calling them except that it completes the proof (إتمام الحجة) against them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: أَلَمْ نَخْلُقْكُمْ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ (Did We not create you from a mean water), he said: "Stinking." فَجَعَلْناهُ فِي قَرارٍ مَكِينٍ (then We lodged it in a secure abode), he said: "In the womb." As for His saying: إِلى قَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ (until a known term), he says: "The end of the appointed term (الأجل)."

I say: In Usul al-Kafi, in a narration from Abu al-Hasan al-Madi (peace be upon him), he applied His saying: أَلَمْ نُهْلِكِ الْأَوَّلِينَ (Did We not destroy the former generations?) to those who denied the Messengers regarding obedience to the Successors (الأوصياء), and His saying: ثُمَّ نُتْبِعُهُمُ الْآخِرِينَ (Then We will make the latter ones follow them) to those who committed crimes against the Family of Muhammad (peace be upon him). This is despite some disorder in the text of the report, and it belongs to the category of esoteric application (الجري) rather than literal exegesis (التفسير).

In the same source: Regarding His saying: أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ الْأَرْضَ كِفاتاً أَحْياءً وَأَمْواتاً (Did We not make the earth a receptacle for the living and the dead?), he said: "The kifat are the dwellings." It is said that the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), upon his return from Siffin, looked at the graves and said: "These are the kifat of the dead," meaning their dwellings. Then he looked at the houses of Kufa and said: "These are the kifat of the living." Then he recited His saying: أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ الْأَرْضَ كِفاتاً أَحْياءً وَأَمْواتاً.

I say: It is narrated in al-Maʿani, with its chain of transmission from Hammad, from Abu ʿAbdillah (peace be upon him), that he looked at the graves and mentioned a similar tradition.

In the same source: Regarding His saying: وَجَعَلْنا فِيها رَواسِيَ شامِخاتٍ (and set in it high, firm mountains), he said: "Lofty mountains."

In the same source: Regarding His saying: انْطَلِقُوا إِلى ظِلٍّ ذِي ثَلاثِ شُعَبٍ (Proceed toward a triple-forked shadow), he said: "In it are three forks of fire." Regarding His saying: إِنَّها تَرْمِي بِشَرَرٍ كَالْقَصْرِ (Indeed it throws sparks like [huge] castles), he said: "The sparks of the Fire are like castles and mountains."

In the same source: Regarding His saying: إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي ظِلالٍ وَعُيُونٍ (Indeed the God-conscious will be amid shades and springs), he said: "In shades of light, more radiant than the sun."

In al-Majmaʿ, regarding His saying: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ارْكَعُوا لا يَرْكَعُونَ (When they are told, ‘Bow down,’ they do not bow down), Muqatil said: "It was revealed concerning Thaqif when the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) commanded them to pray. They said: 'We will not bend down (لا ننحني),' and in another narration: 'We do not bend down, for that is a disgrace (سبة) upon us.' So he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'There is no good in a religion that has no bowing (ركوع) and prostration (سجود).'"

I say: The correspondence of this story—which occurred after the Migration (الهجرة)—to the verse is somewhat obscure.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding the previous verse, he said: "And when it is said to them: 'Take the Imam as a guardian (تولوا الإمام),' they do not take him as a guardian."

I say: This is from the category of esoteric application (الجري) rather than literal exegesis (التفسير).
